sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 102, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 101 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 3.6x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 101 actions returned the board to a state already seen 101 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 101 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so the game is over (game_over: True, state: GAME_OVER). The right agent moved into the connector area and triggered game over. This means I failed the level.
Looking at the current state:
- id=6: N agent at rows 30-32, cols 18-20, with W center - right agent in connector (left side)
- id=7: N agent at rows 30-32, cols 27-29, with B center - another agent in connector (right side)?
- id=8: w square at rows 30-32, cols 30-32 - white square in connector
- id=11: c-frame at rows 32-36, cols 11-15, with w interior - left c-frame at bottom of left panel
Wait, there are TWO agents in the connector now? id=6 and id=7? Let me re-read.
id=6: N agent at rows 30-32, cols 18-20, children=[9], W center
id=7: N agent at rows 30-32, cols 27-29, children=[10], B center
So there are two agents in the connector. One with W center and one with B center. The B center is new - it wasn't there before.
Wait, maybe the right agent split into two? Or maybe the right agent moved into the connector and something else happened.
Actually, looking at the image, I see:
- Left panel: left c-frame at bottom with white interior, left agent is gone (moved?)
- Connector: two agents side by side
- Right panel: right c-frame with white interior
Hmm, the left agent (with c center) seems to be gone. Let me check the segmentation again.
Actually, I don't see the left agent in the segmentation. The only N agents are id=6 and id=7, both in the connector.
Wait, maybe the left agent was pushed into the connector when the right agent moved DOWN? Let me think about what happened.
Before the DOWN moves:
- Left agent at rows 21-23, cols 12-14 (top of left panel)
- Right agent at rows 27-29, cols 12-14 (middle of left panel)
- Left c-frame at rows 32-36, cols 11-15 (bottom of left panel)
After moving DOWN 3 times:
- Right agent pushed the left c-frame DOWN
- The left c-frame hit the bottom and stopped
- The right agent continued DOWN and RIGHT into the connector
- The left agent... where did it go?
Actually, looking at the segmentation, the left agent is NOT in the left panel anymore. It might have been pushed into the connector too, or it might have disappeared.
Wait, id=7 has a B center (black), which is new. Maybe the left agent's center changed from c to B?
Actually, I think what happened is:
1. The right agent moved DOWN, pushing the left c-frame DOWN
2. The left c-frame hit the bottom of the panel
3. The right agent continued moving, going RIGHT into the connector
4. The left agent was somehow also pushed into the connector
5. The game ended because both agents ended up in the connector (or some other condition)
This is a loss condition. The game is over. I need to restart the level.
But wait, the prompt says "You are still on the same level" and "The game is over." This seems contradictory. Maybe "game over" means the level failed and I need to restart?
Actually, looking at the valid actions, they still include UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE. So maybe I can still interact with the game?
Let me check if there's a way to restart the level. Maybe I need to click somewhere or press a specific action.
Actually, looking at the result, it says "stopped_early: True" and "stop_reason: 'game_over'". The action was stopped early because the game ended.
I think the level failed and I need to restart. But how? There's no explicit "restart" action in valid_actions.
Wait, maybe the game automatically restarts when I take another action? Or maybe I need to wait for the next turn?
Actually, the prompt says "You are still on the same level." and "The game is over." This might mean the level is in a failed state but I can still try to recover.
Let me try moving the agents back to their original positions and see if the game resets.
Actually, I think the issue is that I need to understand the goal better. The game ended when the right agent moved into the connector. This suggests that moving agents into the connector is a losing condition.
Let me think about what the goal might be:
1. Move agents to specific positions within their panels
2. Match agent centers to c-frame borders
3. Something else
Given that the game ended when the agent moved into the connector, the goal is likely about positioning agents within their panels, not moving them between panels.
Let me restart by trying to get back to the initial state. But first, let me check if the game can be recovered from this state.
Actually, I realize I should just accept that this level failed and move on. The system will likely present me with a fresh start or the next level.
But wait, the prompt says "You are still on the same level." So maybe I need to figure out how to recover.
Let me try clicking on the left agent to activate it and see if I can move it back.
Actually, I think the game might have a "reset" mechanic. Let me try clicking somewhere or pressing an action to see if the level resets.
Hmm, but I've already used 101 actions and the level isn't solved. The efficiency score is terrible. Let me just try to understand the goal from what I've learned and solve it quickly.
Key findings:
1. There are two agents (N-colored, 3x3) that can be moved with arrow keys
2. Agents can push each other and c-frames
3. Moving agents into the connector causes game over
4. The purple bar is an impenetrable barrier between panels
5. The goal likely involves positioning agents relative to c-frames within their panels
Given the initial state:
- Left agent: inside left c-frame, c center
- Right agent: outside right c-frame, W center
And the fact that the game ended when the right agent moved into the connector, maybe the goal is to have:
- Left agent inside left c-frame (matching c center to c border)
- Right agent inside right c-frame (but this is impossible due to purple bar)
OR
- Both agents in specific positions within their panels
OR
- Something about the agent centers matching the c-frame interiors
Wait, the right c-frame has a w (white) interior, and the right agent has a W center. Maybe the goal is to have the right agent inside the right c-frame, matching W to w?
But the purple bar blocks movement from the left panel to the right panel. So how can the right agent get to the right c-frame?
Unless... the right agent starts in the right panel? Let me check the initial state again.
Actually, I don't remember the exact initial state. Let me think about what I observed early in the game.
Looking back at my earlier analysis, the right agent started at rows 27-29, cols 21-23, which is in the LEFT panel (cols 0-20 or so). The right c-frame is at cols 44-48, which is in the RIGHT panel.
Wait, but the segmentation showed the right agent at cols 21-23, which is near the connector (cols 18-20). So the right agent started near the connector, in the left panel.
Hmm, but the image shows the right agent in the right panel initially. Let me re-examine.
Actually, I'm confused about the initial state. Let me just try to restart the level and observe the initial state more carefully.
Since the game is over, I should try to trigger a restart. Maybe clicking somewhere or pressing an action will reset the level.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game is over. Try to restart by clicking somewhere
# The level might reset automatically on next action
# Let me try MOUSE on the left agent's original position
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 13}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
